coredata.dc:title: In situ synthesis of polychromic up/down-conversion carbon dots/YF3 composites for information encryption and security
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Youfusheng
Chen, Xiao
Guo, Panwang
Xue, Enbo
Tian, Bin
Zheng, Ke
Liang, Jing
Wu, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Polychromic up/down-conversion emissions in carbon dots (CDs) system play a key role in advanced information encryption and security. However, it is difficult to simultaneously achieve tunable up/down-conversion emissions in CDs system owing to the difficulty in preparation of red down-conversion emission and the poor absorption of CDs to near-infrared excitation sources. Herein, red fluorescence (FL) and room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) emissions are obtained by establishing energy transfer processes from blue CDs to the Eu3+/Mn2+. Color-tunable upconversion luminescence (UCL) is achieved by in situ preparation process of rare-earth (Er3+/Yb3+, Tm3+/Yb3+, and Tm3+/Er3+) co-doped YF3 upconversion nanoparticles on CDs/boric acid composites. Based on the mold-filling, deposition, handwriting and screen-printing methods, various stable security patterns and characters composed of multicolor emissions of FL, UCL and RTP by switching excitation wavelength and external thermal stimulation, showing great potential for information encryption and security. This work paves the way for the color conversion of FL, RTP and UCL in CDs system and the construction of advanced information encryption and security technologies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polychromic emissions
Energy transfer
Red emission
Up/down-conversion
Information encryption and security

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macao Joint Innovation Funding Project of Guangdong Science and Technology Program
Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National High-Level Talents Special Support Program , Special Project on Knowledge Innovation of Wuhan ( whkxjsj027 ), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( 2042021kf0226 , 2042022kf1052 ), Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macao Joint Innovation Funding Project of Guangdong Science and Technology Program ( 2020A0505140004 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2022TQ0239 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The TEM image, HRTEM image and particle sizes of CDs. (b) The UV–Vis, excitation (λem = 448 nm) and emission spectra (λex = 365 nm) as well as actual pictures of CDs. (c) The XRD results of BA and BA-CDs. (d) The TEM image of BA-CDs. (e) The excitation (λem = 419 nm), FL emission (λex = 365 nm) and RTP emission (removal of λex = 365 nm), as well as actual pictures (inset) of BA-CDs. (f) The FT-IR spectrum of BA-CDs.
(a) The full XPS and high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, (d) O1s and (e) B1s of BA-CDs sample. (f) The proposed mechanisms of FL and RTP emissions.

(a) The schematic diagram of designing energy transfer process from CDs to the emitters. (b) The excitation spectra of BA-CDs-Eu (λem = 617 nm) and BA-CDs-Mn (λem = 665 nm) as well as the FL (λex = 365 nm) and RTP emission (removal of λex = 365 nm) spectra of BA-CDs. (c)–(d) The contrastive FL (λex = 365 nm) and RTP (removal of λex = 365 nm) spectra of BA-CDs and BA-CDs-Eu. (e)–(f) The contrastive FL (λex = 365 nm) and RTP (removal of λex = 365 nm) spectra of BA-CDs and BA-CDs-Mn.

(a)–(c) The UCL spectra and actual pictures (inset) under 980 nm excitation and (d)–(f) the normalized FL (λex = 365 nm) and RTP (removal of λex = 365 nm) spectra of UCL-B-1, UCL-B-2, UCL-G-1, UCL-G-2, UCL-R-1 and UCL-R-2 samples.

(a) The patterns composed of different security inks (I) S-BA-CDs, (II) S-BA-CDs-Eu, (III) S-UCL-B-2, (IV) S-UCL-G-2, (V) S-UCL-R-2 and (VI) ink-CDs by switching UV excitation and using a 980 nm laser. (b) The patterns using the security ink of S-UCL-G-1 under the UV excitation and after removal of the light. (c) The pictures of characters of ancient poetry and letters under 365 nm excitation based on the writable ink. (d) Security patterns obtained by screen-printing way by using ink-CDs, ink-UCL-G-2 and ink-UCL-R-2 as security inks. (e) The printed QD code based on the security ink of ink-UCL-G-2.
The schematic diagram of the synthesis of blue CDs, corresponding powders and security patterns.

head.ce:title: In situ synthesis of polychromic up/down-conversion carbon dots/YF3 composites for information encryption and security

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Printable Functional Materials and Printed Electronics, Research Center for Graphic Communication, Printing and Packaging, Wuhan University, Wuhan, 430072, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Printable Functional Materials and Printed Electronics
Research Center for Graphic Communication, Printing and Packaging

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory of Printable Functional Materials and Printed Electronics, Research Center for Graphic Communication, Printing and Packaging, Wuhan University, Wuhan, 430072, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polychromic up/down-conversion emissions in carbon dots (CDs) system play a key role in advanced information encryption and security. However, it is difficult to simultaneously achieve tunable up/down-conversion emissions in CDs system owing to the difficulty in preparation of red down-conversion emission and the poor absorption of CDs to near-infrared excitation sources. Herein, red fluorescence (FL) and room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) emissions are obtained by establishing energy transfer processes from blue CDs to the Eu3+/Mn2+. Color-tunable upconversion luminescence (UCL) is achieved by in situ preparation process of rare-earth (Er3+/Yb3+, Tm3+/Yb3+, and Tm3+/Er3+) co-doped YF3 upconversion nanoparticles on CDs/boric acid composites. Based on the mold-filling, deposition, handwriting and screen-printing methods, various stable security patterns and characters composed of multicolor emissions of FL, UCL and RTP by switching excitation wavelength and external thermal stimulation, showing great potential for information encryption and security. This work paves the way for the color conversion of FL, RTP and UCL in CDs system and the construction of advanced information encryption and security technologies.

Stable security patterns with polychromic up/down-conversion emissions induced by energy transfer processes reveal great potential in information encryption and security.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The circulation of forged products and the leakage of confidential information have caused huge losses and will further damage the interests of the country, enterprises and individuals [1,2]. Adopting security strategies to effectively combat counterfeit goods and protect information security remains a global challenge. To date, various methods including quick response (QR) code, watermark, barcode, fluorescence (FL) label, and laser holographic have been widely used in the field of actual information protection [3–5]. In contrast, security technology supported by optical materials has become a promising alternative technology due to many advantages such as large information storage capacity, facile light sources, controllable optical properties, cost-effectiveness and so forth [6,7]. It is noteworthy that the security performance of phosphors is particularly prominent. The information encryption technology based on phosphors mainly depends on the luminous color and luminous category of phosphors. Therefore, to obtain better security performance, it is of great significance to develop effective strategies for multicolor emissions and various luminous categories of optical materials. As an emerging optical material, carbon dots (CDs) have shown great potential in information encryption and security because of their color-tunable emission colors, up/down-conversion emissions (fluorescence (FL), room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP), thermally activated decay fluorescence (TADF), and upconversion luminescence (UCL)), as well as the merits of low-toxicity and high photostability [8–12]. However, it is hard to simultaneously achieve polychromatic down-conversion emissions (particularly red emissions) and tunable upconversion emissions in CDs systems, and thus it is difficult to meet the requirements of practical applications.

Various strategies have been adopted to realize multicolor emissions of up/down-conversion luminescence in CDs system [13]. For instance, researchers have reported color-tunable FL emissions of CDs by varying the composition of carbon core, carbon sources, oxidation degree and solvents [14–19]. The multicolor RTP of CDs was obtained by altering the type of substrate and excitation wavelengths [20–22]. Compared with the first two luminescence strategies, the UCL of CDs is more difficult to obtain, and the emission colors of UCL are uncontrollable and not abundant enough in CDs system [23]. Although some excellent studies have been reported, the both achievement of multicolor emissions of up/down-conversion luminescence in a CDs system has rarely been reported, and most of the reported CDs with long-wavelength emissions suffer from the shortcomings of their poor stability, hydrophilic and hydrophobic characteristics [24]. In addition, the current studies reveal that the longer the wavelength of FL emission, the more difficult it is to fabricate the CDs, so most CDs exhibit short-wavelength FL emission, especially blue FL. For the RTP emission color of CDs, since CDs are immobilized in matrix (such as SiO2, B2O3 and polyvinyl alcohol), it is easy to obtain short-wavelength green RTP [25–27]. According to the luminescent mechanism, the RTP emission of CDs should undergo an intersystem crossing (ISC) process from singlet states to triplet states, implying that the lower singlet state is necessary for long-wavelength RTP emission. Note that the width of singlet states is closely related to the defect levels induced by various surface functional groups of CDs [28]. The participation of matrixes often leads to the destruction of the surface structure of CDs, which in turn makes the preparation of RTP CDs with long-wavelength RTP emission very difficult. The above discussions demonstrate that effective strategies that can convert the short-wavelength FL and RTP emissions into their long-wavelength emissions are necessary and important. The energy transfer process is important for inorganic luminescence materials to realize outstanding color conversion [29–31]. Introducing effective energy transfer strategies into CDs system is expected to obtain unexpected emissions of CDs. For instance, Yu group reported the TADF emission of zeolite-confined CDs based on energy transfer process from the blue emissive CDs to the green emissive CDs [32]. Liu group obtains at first multicolor upconversion afterglow through designing energy transfer process from UCL materials to CDs [33]. Hu group realizes tunable RTP based on energy transfer from inorganic trap state of the ZnAl2O4 and B2O3 matrixes to CDs [34,35]. These studies reveal that establishing effective energy transfer processes in CDs/matrixes composite systems is feasible and significant for designing the desirable optical properties in CDs systems.

There are few reports about the UCL of CDs, mainly due to the poor absorption of long-wavelength excitation light by CDs. The reported UCL emission of CDs usually requires a high-energy femtosecond laser as an excitation source, and the UCL emission color of CDs isn't abundant enough [8,23,36]. Learning from the lanthanides doped UCL materials, the sensitizer Yb3+ has a large absorption section to 980 nm laser and its absorbed energy can be effectively transferred to the activators (such as the Er3+ and Tm3+) through energy transfer processes to achieve multicolor UCL emissions [29]. Introducing the lanthanide ions into CDs systems is expected to achieve the color-tunable UCL emissions. Previous studies have demonstrated that the crystalline field around the activators and sensitizers plays a key role in UCL. The CDs systems cannot provide a suitable crystalline field for the activators and sensitizers due to the rotation and vibration of various chemical bonds. Additionally, the doping content of metal ions in CDs systems is low for the large differences in electronic configuration and physicochemical properties between metal ions and carbon element [37]. Thereby, the method of directly doping the lanthanides into the CDs systems is still hard to obtain the fascinating UCL emissions. How to implement accessible multicolor UCL into CDs system still puzzles the research community. The composite is attractive because a composite commonly possesses the advantages of each component, and some unexpected combinatorial properties may be concentrated in composite systems [38,39]. The in situ synthesis method is an effective strategy to prepare various composites with integrated properties of different components. Introducing UCL phosphors composed of different activators and sensitizers as well as a matrix into CDs systems through in situ synthesis method is expected to achieve multicolor UCL in CDs systems by manipulating energy transfer processes.

As shown in Scheme 1 , the CDs with blue emission were prepared through a hydrothermal reaction using citric acid (CA) and urea as raw materials. By immobilizing the as-prepared CDs in the H3BO4 (BA) matrix, corresponding powders with blue FL and green RTP emissions were formed. The rare long-wavelength red FL and RTP emissions were obtained through energy transfer processes between the blue CDs and the Eu3+/Mn2+. Multicolor UCL emissions were achieved in the CDs system when the Er3+/Yb3+, Tm3+/Yb3+, and Tm3+/Er3+ co-doped YF3 upconversion nanoparticles were in situ synthesized on the CDs/BA composite. The security inks composed of different luminescence materials and thickening agents were prepared to construct multilevel security patterns. The security patterns possess polychromatic FL, RTP and UCL and interestingly the controllable RTP output of security patterns was exhibited by applying an external thermal stimulation. The proposed energy transfer processes between the CDs and metal ions provide a good reference for tuning FL and RTP properties, and the obtained security patterns with color-tunable FL, RTP and UCL exhibit great potential in advanced information encryption and security.

To obtain the optimal luminescence properties, the diverse ratios of CA to urea are applied. FL spectra in Fig. S1 reveal that the sample with a ratio of 1:4 exhibits the highest FL emission intensity. The morphology of CDs characterized by TEM, as shown in Fig. 1 (a), reveals that CDs possess a small size with an average particle size of 3.18 nm. The HRTEM demonstrates the crystal plane spacing of CDs is 0.21 nm, corresponding to the facet (100) of graphite. Fig. 1(b) presents the UV–Vis, excitation and emission spectra of CDs. CDs possess an obvious absorption peak at 332 nm and a broad peak in visible region, which are related to the n-π* transition of CO/CN and surface states of CDs [40,41]. Two excitation peaks at 292 nm and 390 nm are detected by monitoring 448 nm emission. Owing to more intense background interference of 395 nm than 365 nm in security field, 365 nm is selected as excitation light. CDs possess a blue emission peaking at 448 nm under the 365 nm excitation, which is further determined by inset images. To both achieve FL and RTP emissions in CDs system, the BA is selected as matrix to provide a confinement effect around CDs. XRD results of raw BA and BA-CDs sample are depicted in Fig. 1(c), demonstrating that the BA-CDs sample still retains the BA phase corresponding to the standard card (JCPDS No. 30–0199). The SEM image of BA-CDs in Fig. S2 reveals BA-CDs have irregular shapes. The TEM image of BA-CDs in Fig. 1(d) demonstrates that CDs are successfully immobilized within the BA host. By altering the contents of CDs in Fig. S3, the BA-CDs sample containing 2 mL of CDs exhibits the optimal FL and RTP emission intensity. The excitation, FL emission and RTP emission spectra of the optimal sample are depicted in Fig. 1(e). The optimal excitation wavelength is at 346 nm. The optimal BA-CDs sample possesses a blue FL emission peaking at 419 nm under 365 nm excitation and a green RTP emission peaking at 503 nm after removal of the excitation light, which are revealed by its actual pictures in inset images. The CIE coordinates of FL and RTP emissions in Fig. S4 are changed from (0.176, 0.145) to (0.263, 0.433) by switching the 365 nm excitation light, demonstrating successful achievement of FL and RTP by using BA as carrier material.

To reveal the surface groups and component of CDs, FT-IR and XPS tests are used. As revealed in Fig. 1(f), the stretching vibration of C–H band is found at 2852 cm−1 and 2925 cm−1 [16,42]. The peaks at 3216 cm−1 and 3440 cm−1 are related to the N–H and O–H stretching vibration, respectively [43]. The vibration of CC band is found at the peaks of 1623 cm−1 and 1452 cm−1 [44]. The peaks at 1027 cm−1 and 1714 cm−1 are ascribed to the C–N and CO bands, respectively [18]. The B–O band is observed with a peak at 1195 cm−1 [45]. The peak at 781 cm−1 belongs to C–Cl band [46,47]. Fig. 2 (a) shows the full XPS spectrum of BA-CDs, demonstrating the existence of C, N, O and B elements, and corresponding proportions are 33.91%, 1.6%, 37.75% and 26.74%, respectively. High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s, O1s and B1s are depicted in Fig. 2(b)–(e). The high-resolution C1s spectrum is classified into four parts, containing CC/C–C (284.8 eV), C–O/C–N (286.2 eV), CO (287.1 eV) and COOH (288.7 eV) [17]. The high-resolution N1s spectrum consists of N–H (401.2 eV) and graphitic N (402.3 eV) are found [48,49]. The high-resolution O1s spectrum is composed of C–O (534.2 eV) and CO (533.0 eV) [50]. The high-resolution B1s spectrum consists of B–C (193.1 eV) and B–O (194.3 eV) [51]. The above analysis results demonstrate that the CDs are mainly functionalized by hydroxyl, amino and hydrogen groups. Relying on optical properties and structural composition of BA-CDs, the FL and PL mechanisms of BA-CDs are speculated in Fig. 2(f). In a stimulated state, the absorbed energy is migrated from the singlet state S0 to the high-lying singlet states Sn. FL emission is obtained through energy transfer processes from the Sn to S0. RTP emission cannot be observed in a liquid state owing to large energy consumption caused by the vibration of different chemical bonds. Benefiting from the confinement effect of BA host, RTP emission is discovered from the BA-CDs system through the intersystem crossing (ISC) process from the singlet to triplet states (Sn → Tn) and energy transfer from the triplet states to ground state (Tn → S0) [52].

As discussed above, owing to the confinement effect of BA host, energy can be transferred between different states. Thereby, we speculate that the energy absorbed by CDs also can be transferred to other low-lying energy levels from a suitable emitter to achieve long-wavelength emissions, especially the red emission, as shown in Fig. 3 (a). Learning from the previous reports, the Eu3+ and Mn2+ are of great importance in red emission owing to the energy transfer processes of 5D0 → 7FJ (J = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 and 6) of Eu3+ and 4T1 → 6A1 of Mn2+ [53]. Thereby, the Eu3+ and Mn2+ are selected to construct energy transfer processes from CDs to them to achieve red emission. The XRD result and SEM image of representative BA-CDs-Eu sample are revealed in Fig. S5(a)–(b). The as-prepared BA-CDs-Eu mainly possesses the BA phase structure and an irregular morphology. To reveal the valence state of europium and manganese dopants, the full XPS of BA-CDs-Eu and BA-CDs-Mn and the high-resolution spectra of europium and manganese elements are measured and shown in Fig. S6(a)–(d). The europium and manganese elements are detected in the two as-prepared samples, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of the 3d orbit of europium can be divided into four parts, including Eu3d3/2 (1165.5 eV), Eu3d3/2 (1155.3 eV), Eu3d5/2 (1135.6 eV) and Eu3d5/2 (1125.5 eV). The high-resolution XPS spectrum of manganese element reveals that the 2p orbit is split into Mn2p1/2 (654.1 eV) and Mn2p1/2 (642.0 eV). XPS results demonstrate that the europium and manganese elements doped in BA-CDs are trivalent and divalent, respectively.

The spectral overlaps between the emission spectra of CDs and the excitation spectra of Eu3+ and Mn2+ are significant for achieving energy transfer from the CDs donators to the Eu3+ and Mn2+ acceptors. As depicted in Fig. 3(b), the excitation spectra of BA-CDs-Eu and BA-CDs-Mn samples are tested by monitoring 617 nm and 665 nm emission peaks, respectively. The FL and RTP emission spectra of BA-CDs sample are overlapped with excitation spectra of BA-CDs-Eu and BA-CDs-Mn samples, indicating the possibility of energy transfer between CDs and Eu3+/Mn2+. The contrastive FL emission spectra of BA-CDs and BA-CDs-Eu samples are revealed in Fig. 3(c). By adding the Eu3+, the specific emission peaks belonging to the 5D0 → 7FJ (J = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 and 6) of Eu3+ are found in the BA-CDs-Eu sample upon 365 nm excitation. As depicted in Fig. 3(d), compared with the RTP spectrum of BA-CDs sample, the sharply red emission peaks of Eu3+ are newly increased after removal of the 365 nm excitation. The CIE coordinates of the FL and RTP of BA-CDs-Eu in Fig. S7 are (0.201, 0.129) and (0.508, 0.385), respectively, which reveal the FL and RTP emissions severally tend to the purple and red regions. Under a higher Eu3+ content, the emission intensity of FL and RTP emission peaks of the Eu3+ are higher than that of CDs under the 365 nm excitation and after removal of the excitation in Fig. S8(a). Fig. S8(b) reveals that the CIE coordinates of FL and RTP of BA-CDs-Eu sample with a high content are (0.291, 0.194) and (0.600, 0.361), respectively. In addition, the similar performance changes of FL and RTP are found in the BA-CDs-Mn sample as incorporating with Mn2+. As shown in Fig. 3(e)–(f), a broad FL emission peak originated from the 4T1 → 6A1 transition of the Mn2+ is discovered in BA-CDs-Mn upon 365 nm stimulation, and a broad RTP emission peak is also obtained after the removal of 365 nm excitation. As depicted in Fig. S7, the CIE coordinates of FL and RTP emissions of BA-CDs-Mn are located at (0.246, 0.182) and (0.576, 0.394), respectively, which demonstrate the emission color is changed toward long-wavelength region by introducing the Mn2+. The changes in spectral profiles of FL and RTP of BA-CDs, BA-CDs-Eu and BA-CDs-Mn samples reveal that energy transfer processes from CDs to the Eu3+/Mn2+ have occurred. The above results demonstrate that the designing strategy on introducing Eu3+/Mn2+ into BA-CDs system to achieve red FL and RTP emissions is successful and feasible. The energy transfer mechanisms between CDs and Eu3+/Mn2+ are illustrated in Fig. S9. Excitation energy absorbed by CDs promotes the increase in the number of the electrons on the singlet and triplet states. The absorbed energy can be divided into two parts, including converted energy from the singlet and triplet states to ground state for achieving FL and RTP emissions of CDs and migrated energy from CDs to the excited states of Eu3+ and Mn2+. Various energy transfer processes from the 5D0 to 7FJ (J = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 and 6) of Eu3+ and the 4T1 to 6A1 transition of Mn2+ lead to long-wavelength FL and RTP emissions.

To realize UCL in CDs system, UCL materials are introduced into the CDs system through a in situ preparation process. The XRD results of UCL-B-1 and UCL-B-2 are revealed in Fig. S10(a). The diffraction peaks of UCL-B-1 match with the standard data of the NH4Cl (JCPDS No. 07-0007), NaCl (JCPDS No. 05–0628) and YF3 (JCPDS No. 32–1431). After calcination at 400 °C for 4 h, the diffraction peaks of UCL-B-2 agree well with the reference data of the YF3. The SEM image of UCL-B-2 in Fig. S10(b) demonstrates that the smaller YF3 particles are in situ grown on the surface of large irregular BA-CDs particles. The effects of calcination temperature and time on the UCL intensity of BA-CDs doped with Tm3+ and Yb3+ are tested and shown in Figs. S11(a)–(b). As the calcination temperature increase from 250 °C to 450 °C at interval of 50 °C, the strongest emission intensity is obtained at 400 °C. The optimal calcination time is at 4 h, as the calcination time alters from 2 h to 10 h at interval of 2 h. The UCL-B-2, UCL-G-2 and UCL-R-2 were heated at 400 °C for 4 h. The UCL spectra of UCL-B-1, UCL-B-2, UCL-G-1, UCL-G-2, UCL-R-1 and UCL-R-2 samples are revealed in Fig. 4 (a)–(c). Contrastively, the higher emission intensities of emission peaks are observed after a calcination process. For the UCL-B-2, UCL-G-2 and UCL-R-2 samples, UCL with blue, green and red color are observed under the 980 nm excitation, which are confirmed by actual pictures and corresponding CIE coordinates of (0.153, 0.108), (0.334, 0.649) and (0.597, 0.391), as shown in Fig. S12.The blue and red emissions of UCL-B-2 are ascribed to the 1G4 → 3H6, 1G4 → 3F4 and 3F2,3 → 3H6 processes, respectively. The green and red emissions of UCL-G-2 are ascribed to the 2H11/2/4S3/2 → 4I15/2 and 4F9/2 → 4I15/2 processes, respectively. Compared the UCL spectral profile of UCL-R-2 with that of UCL-B-2 and UCL-G-2 samples, the UCL spectral profile UCL-R-2 is similar to that of UCL-G-2 sample, which reveals energy transfer processes between Er3+ and Tm3+ contribute to red emission.

To reveal energy transfer processes in the couples of Tm3+/Yb3+, Er3+/Yb3+, and Tm3+/Er3+, the UCL spectra of UCL-B-2, UCL-G-2 and UCL-R-2 are tested under altered excitation power and corresponding log-log plots between excitation power and UCL intensity are shown in Figs. S13(a)–(c). The slope values of one blue and two red emissions of UCL-B-2 are 1.82 ± 0.02, 1.68 ± 0.04 and 1.42 ± 0.03, respectively. The slope values of 1G4 → 3H6 and 1G4 → 3F4 transitions are lower than 2, which should be ascribed to energy consumption caused by non-radiative processes [54]. The three-/two-photon processes are response to blue and red emissions of UCL-B-2, respectively. The emissions of UCL-G-2 are ascribed to two-photon processes because the slope values are 1.71 ± 0.06, 1.54 ± 0.04 and 1.66 ± 0.04, respectively. For the UCL-R-2, the slope values are 1.52 ± 0.10, 1.71 ± 0.09 and 1.78 ± 0.08, which demonstrates two green and one red emissions are caused by two-photon processes. The proposed UCL processes for multicolor emissions of UCL-B-2, UCL-G-2 and UCL-R-2 are presented in Fig. S14. In the Tm3+/Yb3+ co-activated system, the absorbed energy by the Yb3+ is transferred to the Tm3+, making the electrons on the 3H6 level changed onto the high-lying 3H5, 3F2,3 and 1G4 levels. The non-radiative processes including the 3H5 → 3F4 and 3F2,3 → 3H4 are involved. Blue and red emissions are obtained as the electrons on the high-lying 1G4 and 3F2,3 levels are back to the ground state 3H6. In the Er3+/Yb3+ co-activated system, the Yb3+ absorbs excited energy and then transfers them to the Er3+, realizing the changes of electronic population on the high-lying 4F7/2 level. After undergoing two non-radiative processes from the 4F7/2 to 2H11/2/4S3/2 levels, two green emissions of the Er3+ are obtained through the 2H11/2/4S3/2 → 4I15/2 transitions. Red emission is obviously observed for increased electronic population on the 4F9/2 through the two channels including a non-radiative process of the 4S3/2 → 4F9/2 and an energy transfer process of the 4I13/2 → 4F9/2. In the Tm3+/Er3+ co-activated system, red emission intensity is higher than that of green emission. The red emission of Tm3+/Er3+ co-doped system has a higher slope of 1.78 ± 0.08 than that (1.66 ± 0.04) of the Er3+/Yb3+ co-activated system. Based on the above results, it can be inferred that the Tm3+ may act a medium to achieve large electronic population on the 4F9/2 state. The electronic population on the 4F9/2 state may be improved by a cross-relaxation process of CR1: 1G4 (Er3+) + 4I15/2 (Er3+) → 3F4 (Tm3+) + 4F9/2 (Er3+) and a continuous energy transfer process of 3H5 (Tm3+) → 4I13/2 (Er3+) → 4F9/2 (Er3+) [55–57]. As a result, red CUL is obtained as the 4F9/2 → 4I15/2 process occurs.

The normalized FL and RTP spectra of UCL-B-1, UCL-B-2, UCL-G-1, UCL-G-2, UCL-R-1 and UCL-R-2 are shown in Fig. 4(d)–(f). The UCL-B-1, UCL-G-1 and UCL-R-1 possess blue FL under 365 nm excitation and blue-green RTP after removal of 365 nm excitation. After undergoing a calcination treatment, the FL emission color of UCL-B-2, UCL-G-2 and UCL-R-2 is changed toward white region under 365 nm excitation due to the longer range of tails and the RTP emission color of them is presented as cyan-green color after removal of 365 nm excitation. For the UCL-B-1, UCL-B-2, UCL-G-1, UCL-G-2, UCL-R-1 and UCL-R-2 composites, multicolor emissions of FL, RTP and UCL make them attractive in the information encryption and security fields.

The emission color and luminous categories of optical materials are key factors for constricting high-level information encryption and security technologies. To reveal the potential of as-obtained optical materials, security inks composed of these optical materials and thickening agents are used to prepare various patterns through the mold filling, deposition, handwriting and screen-printing methods. Among these patterns, different parts of them are made up of optical materials with different optical features. In Fig. 5 (a), a complete pattern including flowers and branches with various colors can be clearly observed under UV excitation. Only the flowers are visible after turning off UV excitation. Multicolor UCL are observed upon 980 nm laser stimulation. Subsequently, the security ink of S-UCL-G-1 is deposited on a cuboid mold in Fig. 5(b). Using UV light as excitation source, blue FL and cyan-green RTP emissions are observed. Interestingly, the RTP emission disappears as the as-prepared sample is exposed to the atmosphere for 1 day, and the RTP is restored after the heat treatment for 30 min at 80 °C in an oven. The RTP disappears after a storage again. The writable security ink is essential for the document encryption field. As revealed in Fig. 5(c), the encrypted characters of ancient poetry and letters on an envelope and letter paper are obtained by writing with a pen containing the writable security ink. Blue FL emission of these characters is decrypted under UV excitation, which reveals potential application in document protection. To broaden the applications of information encryption and security, screen printing technique is used to print QD code and lotus flowers in Fig. 5(d). The FL emission of the QD code is detected. By means of overprinting (three times), an integrated pattern including lotus flowers and leaves is obtained, which is discernible under UV excitation. An incomplete pattern that lacks the lotus in full bloom is presented after removal of UV excitation and that with green and red UCL emissions is realized upon 980 nm laser stimulation. Similarly, the ink-UCL-G-2 is used to print a QD code, as shown in Fig. 5(e). The printed QD code possesses multicolor emissions of different luminous categories, including blue FL, cyan-green RTP and green UCL. To reveal the stability, the printed QD code is collected at different times by switching the UV excitation. As the QD code is exposed to the atmosphere for 4 days, it exhibits blue FL and no RTP emissions. It is noteworthy that the RTP emission of printed QD code appears after undergoing a heat gun provided by a hair dryer (1800 W). Similar optical performances of FL and RTP emissions of the printed QD code are observed after the printed QD code is stored for the thirty and one hundred days. The optical performances and stability of security patterns reveal great potential of the as-prepared optical materials in practical information encryption and security.

In summary, CDs with blue FL emission were gained by a hydrothermal reaction of CA and urea. The blue FL and green RTP emissions of BA-CDs were realized by immobilizing blue CDs into BA host. The metal ions of Eu3+/Mn2+ were added in the CDs-immobilized process to tune the emission colors of FL and RTP, achieving the red FL and RTP emissions based on energy transfer processes from CDs to Eu3+/Mn2+. UCL with blue, green and red colors were obtained based on energy transfer among Er3+, Tm3+ and Yb3+ as the YF3 upconversion nanoparticles co-doped with Er3+/Yb3+, Tm3+/Yb3+, and Tm3+/Er3+ were in situ grown on the BA-CDs particles. The as-prepared security patterns and characters were obtained by different fabrication methods including the mold filling, deposition, handwriting and screen-printing methods. These patterns and characters exhibit the multicolor emissions of FL, RTP and UCL by applying different excitation sources and external thermal stimulation, which demonstrate great performances in information protection. This work provides a good reference to convert short-wavelength FL and RTP emissions into the longer-wavelength FL and RTP emissions, and offers abundant optical properties to construct advanced information encryption and security.

Youfusheng Wu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Formal Analysis, Writing - Original Draft. Xiao Chen: Software, Investigation, Visualization. Panwang Guo: Software, Validation. Enbo Xue, Bin Tian, Ke Zheng and Jing Liang: Investigation. Wei Wu: Conceptualization, Funding Acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing - Review & Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National High-Level Talents Special Support Program, Special Project on Knowledge Innovation of Wuhan (whkxjsj027), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2042021kf0226, 2042022kf1052), Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macao Joint Innovation Funding Project of Guangdong Science and Technology Program (2020A0505140004), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2022TQ0239).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2023.120013.
